message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Rock didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the lighthouse keeper, the silence was a companion as constant as the rhythmic *thrum-click* of the great Fresnel lens above him. He had kept the light for thirty years, watching ships pass like ghosts in the mist, never speaking to a soul unless the radio crackled with a routine weather update.

It was a Tuesday, the kind of gray, heavy day that made the sea look like bruised iron, when he found it.

He was walking the perimeter of the rock, checking for barnacle growth on the foundation, when a glint of green glass caught his eye near the tide line. It wasn’t washed up debris; it was a bottle, sealed with wax that had hardened into a dark, amber scar. The glass was thick, old-fashioned, the kind used for spirits in the early 1900s.

Elias picked it up. It was heavy. He shook it. A single sheet of paper rattled inside.

Back in the keeper’s quarters, the smell of salt and old oil hanging in the air, he pried the cork loose with his pocketknife. The paper was brittle, yellowed at the edges, but the ink was still sharp, written in a precise, looping hand.

*To the Keeper of Blackwood,*

*If you are reading this, the mechanism has failed. The light is not broken, but it is blind. You have been keeping a light for a ship that sank in 1923. The *Aethelgard*. It did not crash on the rocks. It was scuttled. The cargo was not gold, but something far heavier. Look beneath the third step of the spiral staircase. The truth is buried with the guilt.*

*— J.M.*
